ko: 그렇게 하면 비둘기집 원리를 적용해서 100과 100만큼 차이나는 소수 쌍이 있다는 걸 증명할 수 있어.

en: And that gives you a shot at proving by applying some sort of pigeonhole principle that there's pairs of primes that are just only 100 and 100
ko: 하지만 쌍둥이 소수 추측을 증명하려면 거의 소수 안의 소수 밀도를 50까지 올려야 해.

en: apart
ko: 50까지 가면 쌍둥이 소수를 얻을 수 있어. 하지만 안타깝게도 장벽이 있어.

en: But in order to prove the twin prime conjecture you need to get the density of primes inside the almost primes up to a threshold of 50
ko: 어떤 좋은 거의 소수 집합을 선택하든 소수의 밀도가 50을 넘을 수 없어.

en: Once you get up to 50 you would get twin primes But unfortunately there are barriers
ko: 이걸 패리티 장벽이라고 해.

en: We know that no matter what kind of good set of almost primes you pick the density of primes can never get above 50 It's
ko: 그래서 이 장벽을 뚫는 방법을 찾고 싶어.

en: called the parity barrier
ko: 이걸 뚫으면 쌍둥이 소수 추측뿐만 아니라 여러 수론 문제들이 열릴 거야.

en: And I would love to find yeah so one of my long term dreams is to find a way to breach that barrier
ko: 현재의 기술로는 이 이론적 패리티 장벽을 넘는 게 필요해.

en: Because it would open up not only the twin prime conjecture the go back conjecture and many other problems in number theory are currently blocked

en: because our current techniques would require

en: going beyond this theoretical parity barrier It's like going fast in the speed of light Yeah so we should
say a twin prime conjecture one of the biggest problems in the history of mathematics go back conjecture also
They feel like next door neighbors

en: measure and it's called the Riemann zeta function and it fluctuates up and down But in some sense as you keep averaging more

en: and more if you sample more and more the fluctuation should go down as if they were random And there's a very precise way to quantify that And

en: the Riemann hypothesis is a very elegant way that captures this
ko: 같은 의미로 소수를 특정 곱셈적 의미로 측정하면

en: work

en: fascinating Yeah yeah yeah What to you is most mysterious about the prime numbers

en: That's a good question So conjecturally we have a good model of them I mean as I said I mean they have certain
ko: 좋은 질문이야. 추측적으로 우리는 그들에 대한 좋은 모델을 가지고 있어. 내가 말했듯이, 그들은 특정한 패턴을 가지고 있어.

en: patterns like the primes are usually odd for instance but apart from these obvious patterns they behave very randomly And just assuming
ko: 예를 들어 소수는 보통 홀수야. 하지만 이런 명백한 패턴 외에도 그들은 매우 무작위로 행동해. 그냥 그렇게 행동한다고 가정하면,

en: that they behave so there's something called the Kramer random model of the primes
ko: 소수의 크레이머 랜덤 모델이라는 게 있어.

en: that after a certain point primes just behave like a random set And there's various slight modifications to this model but this
ko: 어느 시점 이후 소수는 그냥 무작위 집합처럼 행동해. 이 모델에 약간의 수정이 있지만,

en: has been a very good model It matches the numerics It tells us what to predict Like I can tell you with complete certainty the

en: Riemann conjecture is true The random model gives overwhelming odds it is true I just can't prove it Most of our

en: mathematics is optimized for solving things with patterns in them
